summary: Azure RBAC is an authorization system built on Azure Resource Manager that controls who can access Azure resources, what actions they can perform, and at what scope. Access is granted by creating role assignments that combine a security principal, a role definition, and a scope. It uses an additive permissions model, and deny assignments take precedence over role assignments.

key_concepts.explanation: The identity requesting access — can be a user, group, service principal, or managed identity.
A collection of permissions (Actions, NotActions, DataActions, NotDataActions) defining what operations are allowed or excluded.
The boundary at which a role is assigned — can be management group, subscription, resource group, or resource, structured as a parent-child hierarchy.
The binding of a role definition to a security principal at a specific scope; creating it grants access, removing it revokes access.
Explicit blocks on actions that override role assignments; if a deny assignment applies, access is blocked regardless of roles granted.
Predefined roles like Owner, Contributor, Reader, and Virtual Machine Contributor provided by Azure for common access scenarios.
User-defined roles created when built-in roles don't meet organizational needs, allowing fine-grained permission sets.
Calculated as Actions minus NotActions for management, and DataActions minus NotDataActions for data plane; multiple assignments are additive.

exam_focus_points: Role assignments consist of exactly three elements: security principal, role definition, and scope.
Azure RBAC uses an additive model — effective permissions are the sum of all role assignments.
Deny assignments take precedence over role assignments and block access even if a role grants it.
The four scope levels in order: management group > subscription > resource group > resource.
Role assignments are transitive for groups, including nested group memberships.
Effective management permissions = Actions - NotActions; Effective data permissions = DataActions - NotDataActions.
Azure RBAC data (role definitions, assignments, deny assignments) is stored and replicated globally.
RBAC is enforced by Azure Resource Manager, not at the OS or application level.
Managed identities and service principals can be assigned roles just like users and groups.
Conditions in role assignments are evaluated last; if conditions are not met, access is denied.
similar_concepts: Azure Active Directory (Entra ID) roles — for managing AAD itself, not Azure resources
Azure Policy — controls what can be done with resources (governance), not who can access them
Resource locks — prevent changes/deletion but are not an access control mechanism
Shared Access Signatures (SAS) — grants scoped access to storage without RBAC role assignments
Classic subscription administrator roles — legacy model replaced by Azure RBAC
common_mistakes: Confusing Azure RBAC roles with Azure AD (Entra ID) roles — they are separate systems with different scopes.
Thinking permissions are exclusive rather than additive — multiple role assignments stack, not override.
Assuming a higher-scope role assignment can be blocked by removing a lower-scope assignment — deny assignments are needed to block inherited permissions.
Forgetting that deny assignments override role grants — candidates often assume roles always win.
Confusing the Contributor role (cannot manage access) with the Owner role (can manage access and assign roles).
Missing that role assignments apply transitively through nested groups.
Thinking RBAC controls data-plane access by default — some services require explicit data action permissions separate from management permissions.
